hero.title: Compound Interest Calculator
hero.subtitle: Discover how small, consistent investments can grow into significant wealth over time using the mathematical power of compound interest.

formula_explanation: The formula considers two parts: the principal (initial investment) compounding over time, and the future value of a series of monthly contributions.
example: If you invest $10,000 today at a 7% annual return, compounded monthly, and add $500 each month for 10 years, you'll end up with approximately $106,300.

faq.question: What is compound interest?
faq.answer: Compound interest is the interest on savings calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods.

faq.question: How often should I compound?
faq.answer: For most investments, monthly or annual compounding is standard. Daily compounding yields slightly higher returns.

faq.question: How does inflation affect my compound returns?
faq.answer: Nominal interest doesn't account for inflation. To find your real return, subtract the annual inflation rate from your compound yield.

faq.question: Is it better to invest more money or invest for longer?
faq.answer: Because of the exponential nature of time, investing earlier for longer periods mathematically outperforms investing much larger amounts later in life.
